1: dabar emet (dābār ʾěmet): 'a true word/message.' Emet (H571, truth/faithfulness/reliability) heads the characterization of the vision — this is not ambiguous prophecy but verified, faithful disclosure. The term tsava gadol (great conflict) signals that the vision concerns cosmic warfare beyond the political surface. Daniel mourning for three weeks (Daniel 10:2-3) is a prolonged preparation for major revelation — cf. Moses on Sinai forty days (Exodus 24:18).
2: hayiti mitabel shloshah shavuim yamim: 'I was mourning for three weeks of days.' Mitabel (H56, mourn): intense sustained grief — Daniel neither eats delicacies nor meat nor wine nor anoints himself for three weeks. The mourning is over the unresolved situation of the exile and the content of the Jeremiah-calculation (Daniel 9). The discipline of extended mourning-prayer as preparation for revelation is a model of prophetic intercession: the body's self-denial corresponds to the spirit's urgent seeking.
3: lechem chamudot lo achalti uvesar vayyayin lo ba bephi: 'I ate no delicacies, no meat or wine came to my mouth, nor did I anoint myself.' The three abstentions — pleasant food, meat, wine, anointing — reverse the court-feast context of Daniel 1:5. The same court setting that Daniel navigated in Daniel 1 is now voluntarily renounced in mourning. Lechem chamudot (desirable/pleasant bread) recalls the chamudot term applied to Daniel himself as the beloved one — here he abstains from what is desirable in order to seek the heavenly beloved.
4: vaani hayiti al-yad hanahar haGadol hu Deqel: 'I was on the bank of the great river, that is, the Tigris.' Deqel (Tigris) — the Tigris rather than the Euphrates as the setting of this vision, as opposed to the Ulai canal of Daniel 8. The great rivers of the ancient Near East are consistently liminal spaces for divine encounter in the prophets: Ezekiel's Chebar canal (Ezekiel 1:1), Ezra's Ahava canal (Ezra 8:21). Rivers mark the boundary between ordinary and sacred space.
5: ish echad lavush badim umotanav chagur betaz Uphaz: 'A man clothed in linen, his waist girded with gold from Uphaz.' Badim (H906, linen): the priestly material par excellence — the high priest wore linen in the holy of holies (Leviticus 16:4); the divine attendants in Ezekiel's vision wore linen (Ezekiel 9:2; Daniel 10:6-7). The figure's clothing signals priestly-divine status. Uphaz: location uncertain — possibly equivalent to Ophir (H211), the OT's gold standard. The Revelation's Son of Man figure (Revelation 1:13) wears the identical garment: endedumenos podere (clothed to the foot) with a golden girdle — a direct Danielic allusion.
6: gevo keTarshish ufanav kemareh baraq ve'einav kelappidei esh: 'His body was like Tarshish stone, his face was like the appearance of lightning, his eyes were like torches of fire.' Tarshish (H8658) is likely beryl/chrysolite — a gleaming, translucent green-blue gem. The sequence of cosmic comparisons — lightning-face, fire-eyes, burnished-bronze limbs, thunder-voice — marks the figure as superhuman, aligned with Ezekiel's divine chariot-throne imagery (Ezekiel 1:7, 13-14, 27). John's vision of the risen Christ (Revelation 1:14-16) replicates these features exactly, identifying the Danielic heavenly figure with the glorified Son of Man.
7: velo ra'u et-hamareh ha'anashim asher hayu imi: 'The men who were with me did not see the vision.' The selectivity of prophetic vision — others nearby yet unseeing — parallels Paul's Damascus road experience (Acts 9:7: men heard the voice but saw nothing; Acts 22:9: saw the light but did not hear the voice). The inability of ordinary perception to receive extraordinary divine disclosure is a consistent feature of biblical theophany: vision is given to those prepared to receive it.
8: ve'ani nisharthi levaddi va'ereh et-hamareh hagedolah hazot velo nithar bi koach vehodi nehavkha alai lemashchit: 'I was left alone and saw this great vision, and no strength remained in me; my radiant appearance was fearfully changed.' Hod (H1935, majesty/radiance/splendor) — the same term used for Moses's face-radiance after encountering YHWH (Exodus 34:29 uses qaran, but hod describes the residual divine glory in figures). The contact with divine majesty depletes human vitality — the body cannot sustain the proximity of the holy. This is not illness but the finite's response to the infinite.
9: va'eshma et-qol devarav uveshomyi et-qol devarav hayiti nirdam: 'I heard the sound of his words, and when I heard the sound of his words I fell into a deep sleep on my face.' Qol devarav (sound/voice of his words): the heavenly voice as overwhelming sonic force — the same as Ezekiel's voice like many waters (Ezekiel 1:24). Nirdam (H7290, fell into deep sleep): not ordinary sleep but the stupor of prophetic trance, parallel to the tardemah (deep sleep) that fell on Abraham at the covenant ceremony (Genesis 15:12). The posture — face to the ground — is the standard theophany response.
10: vehinei yad nag'ah vi vatenioani al-birkai vekaph yadai: 'And behold, a hand touched me and set me trembling on my hands and knees.' The sequence of restoration — touch, rising to knees and hands, then upright (Daniel 10:11) — is a measured reinstatement of the prophet's capacity to receive the revelation. The divine touch that prostrates (overwhelming glory) and restores (enabling grace) is the pattern of all prophetic commissioning: Moses at the burning bush, Isaiah before the throne, Paul on the Damascus road.
11: ish chamudot bina bidevari: 'O man greatly beloved, understand the words that I speak to you.' This is the second occurrence of ish chamudot (man of desirings/beloved man) — Daniel is identified by his belovedness as the qualifier for receiving revelation. Bina (H998, understand/discern) — the imperative to discern, not merely receive, marks the prophetic task. Understanding requires active engagement, not passive reception.
12: al-tira Daniel ki min-hayom harishon asher natata et-libbecha lehavin velehitanot lifnei Eloheicha nishmeu devareicha: 'Do not be afraid, Daniel, for from the first day that you set your heart to understand and to humble yourself before your God, your words have been heard.' Natata et-libbecha (set your heart): lev (heart) as the whole inner life directed toward understanding. Lehitanot (to humble yourself): the reflexive Hitpael of anah — self-humbling as the disposition that opens heavenly access. The revelation was triggered on day one; the twenty-one-day delay was due to cosmic resistance (Daniel 10:13), not divine inattention.
13: sar malkhut Paras omed lenegdi echad ve'esrim yom: 'The prince of the kingdom of Persia stood in opposition against me for twenty-one days.' Sar (H8269, prince/captain/commander) as a cosmic role — each nation has a heavenly sar (angelic guardian) corresponding to its earthly political identity. This doctrine of national angels (cf. Deuteronomy 32:8 LXX, which assigns nations to the sons of God; 1 Enoch 20) explains the geopolitical conflicts of history as having a heavenly dimension. Micha'el achad hasarim hariishonim (Michael, one of the chief princes): Michael is the guardian of Israel (Daniel 12:1), distinguished as a chief-prince among the angelic order.
14: ubate anah lehaskil et asher-yiqreh le'amecha b'acharit hayamim: 'I have come to make you understand what is to happen to your people in the latter days.' B'acharit hayamim (H319 + H3117, in the latter/last days): the prophetic eschatological phrase marking the vision as concerning the end of the present age — the same phrase as Genesis 49:1; Numbers 24:14; Isaiah 2:2; Micah 4:1. The vision is explicitly oriented toward eschatology, not merely near-term political history. Ki od chazot layamim: 'for the vision concerns days to come.'
15: uvedabro immi divarim elleh natati panai artsah ve'nilmeti: 'When he spoke these words to me, I turned my face toward the ground and was speechless.' The prophetic collapse at the content of the heavenly message (not merely the appearance of the heavenly being) deepens the encounter: the revelation itself, not just the revealer, overwhelms. Nilmeti (H481, became silent/speechless) — the speechlessness of profound awe and inadequacy before divine disclosure.
16: venogea kamareh benei adam al-sefatai eftach peh va'adabera: 'One having the appearance of the sons of man touched my lips, and I opened my mouth and spoke.' The touch on the lips echoes Isaiah's commission (Isaiah 6:7 — the coal touched to Isaiah's lips), enabling prophetic speech from one who had been silenced. The restoration of prophetic voice through divine touch is the pattern: God prepares the instrument of his word by touching the very organ of speech.
17: veich yuchal eved Adonai zeh ledabber im-Adonai zeh: 'How can the servant of my Lord speak with this lord of mine?' The double Adonai/Adon creates a reverential rhetorical question: the servant-status of Daniel before the heavenly messenger makes speech feel impossible. The phrase eved Adonai (servant of the Lord) is also the Isaianic designation for the suffering Servant — Daniel's self-identification places him within that tradition of covenant mediation.
18: vayosef vayigga bi kemar'eh ha'adam vayechazqeni: 'And again one having the appearance of a man touched me and strengthened me.' The second touch — now explicitly strengthening (chazaq, H2388) rather than silencing — restores the capacity to receive and transmit the revelation. Chazaq (be strong, strengthen) will recur in 10:19 as the thematic summary of what the heavenly visitation accomplishes for the prophet: Al-tira, shalom lach, chazaq vehitchazaq (Do not fear, peace to you, be strong and strong).
19: al-tira ish chamudot shalom lach chazaq vehitchazaq: 'Do not be afraid, O man greatly beloved. Peace to you; be strong and be strong.' The triple commissioning formula: no fear, peace, and double chazaq. The Hiphil chazaq + Hitpael hitchazaq creates an active and reflexive pairing: both made strong (by God) and actively strengthen yourself. Shalom lach (peace to you) is the greeting of completion and wholeness — the same shalom that marks covenant restoration. Gabriel's parting gift to the prophet is not information but the disposition needed to bear it.
20: ata ashuv lehilachem im-sar Paras veani yotze vehinei sar Yavan ba: 'I will return to fight against the prince of Persia; and when I go out, behold, the prince of Greece will come.' The cosmic geography of heavenly-earthly correspondence: sar Paras (prince of Persia) is replaced by sar Yavan (prince of Greece/Javan) — the Macedonian empire's rise is already visible in heaven before it appears on earth. The angelic battles are the heavenly dimension of the terrestrial empires — Persia falls because its heavenly prince is defeated; Greece rises because its prince overcomes.
21: efes ki agid lach et-harashum bichtav emet ve'ein echad mitchazaq immi al-eleh ki im-Michael sarcem: 'But I will tell you what is inscribed in the book of truth: there is none who contends by my side against these except Michael, your prince.' Ketav emet (book/writing of truth): a heavenly register of predetermined events — the eschatological script that Gabriel reads from. Michael sarcem (Michael your prince) — Israel has its own heavenly guardian, the chief angel Michael (Daniel 12:1), who alone among the heavenly princes stands with Gabriel against the opposing national angels. Israel's protection is personal and angelic.
